questions.question: What does CSS stand for?
questions.explanation: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets. 'Cascading' refers to the rule-resolution system, 'Style' refers to visual presentation, and 'Sheets' refers to the files or blocks containing the styles.

questions.question: Which HTML tag is used to link an external CSS file?
questions.explanation: The <link> tag is used in the HTML <head> to link an external stylesheet: <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct CSS syntax?
questions.explanation: The correct CSS syntax is: selector { property: value; }. So 'body {color: black;}' follows this pattern.

questions.question: Which CSS application method is considered BEST practice for production websites?
questions.explanation: External CSS is best practice because it separates content from presentation, allows browser caching, enables reuse across multiple pages, and simplifies maintenance.

questions.question: What does the 'cascading' in CSS refer to?
options: Styles cascade down from parent to child elements only
The browser follows a set of rules to determine which style applies when multiple rules conflict
CSS files must be arranged in a specific order
Styles can only be applied in a waterfall pattern
questions.explanation: The cascade is the algorithm browsers use to resolve conflicting style declarations based on importance, specificity, and source order.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of inline CSS?
questions.explanation:
Inline CSS uses the style attribute directly on an HTML element:

Hello

questions.question: Which property is NOT inherited by child elements?
questions.explanation: margin is NOT inherited. Properties like color, font-family, and line-height are inherited from parent to child elements.

questions.question: What does the specificity hierarchy look like (highest to lowest)?
questions.explanation: Specificity hierarchy: ID selectors (highest) → Class/attribute/pseudo-class selectors → Element/pseudo-element selectors → Universal selector (lowest).

questions.question: Where should the <link> tag for an external CSS file be placed?
questions.explanation: The <link> tag for external CSS should be placed inside the <head> section to ensure styles load before page content renders.

questions.question: What is the purpose of browser Developer Tools (DevTools) for CSS?
options: To inspect, debug, and test CSS in real-time
To convert CSS to other formats
questions.explanation: DevTools allow developers to inspect elements, view applied CSS rules, edit styles in real-time, debug layout issues, and test responsive designs.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly applies multiple declarations to a selector?
questions.explanation: Multiple declarations are separated by semicolons. Each declaration has format 'property: value;'. Option 0 is the only one with correct syntax.

questions.question: What happens if two CSS rules have the same specificity?
options: The rule that appears later in the source code wins
The rule that appears first wins
questions.explanation: When specificity is equal, the cascade uses source order: the rule that appears later (closer to the bottom) in the stylesheet wins.

questions.question: What is the 'separation of concerns' principle in web development?
options: HTML handles structure, CSS handles presentation, JavaScript handles behavior
Each web page should be built by a separate team
CSS files must be in a separate folder from HTML files
Web design should be separated from web development
questions.explanation: Separation of concerns means HTML is responsible for structure/content, CSS for presentation/styling, and JavaScript for behavior/interactivity.

questions.question: Which CSS selector has the LOWEST specificity?
questions.explanation: The universal selector (*) has the lowest specificity (0-0-0-0). Element selectors (div) are 0-0-0-1, classes are 0-0-1-0, and IDs are 0-1-0-0.

questions.question: What does the 'rel="stylesheet"' attribute do in the <link> tag?
options: Defines the relationship between the current document and the linked file as a stylesheet
Reveals the location of the CSS file
Renders the linked file as a style sheet
questions.explanation: The 'rel' attribute defines the relationship between the current document and the linked resource. 'rel="stylesheet"' tells the browser the linked file is a stylesheet.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a valid way to apply CSS?
questions.explanation: There is no 'css' HTML attribute. CSS can be applied via external <link>, internal <style>, or inline style attribute.

questions.question: What is a 'user-agent stylesheet'?
options: A CSS file created by a user
The default styles built into a web browser
A stylesheet that manages user authentication
questions.explanation: User-agent stylesheets are the default styles that browsers apply to HTML elements (e.g., default margins on <body>, default font sizes for headings).

questions.question: What does 'FOUC' stand for in web development?
questions.explanation: FOUC (Flash of Unstyled Content) is a brief moment when a page appears with default browser styles before the CSS loads. Placing <link> in <head> helps prevent it.

questions.question: Which organization maintains the CSS specifications?
questions.explanation: The W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) maintains and develops CSS specifications. Håkon Wium Lie first proposed CSS in 1994 while at CERN.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a CSS reset or normalize.css?
options: To make all HTML elements invisible
To reduce cross-browser inconsistencies by normalizing default styles
To remove all CSS from a page
questions.explanation: CSS resets and normalize.css reduce browser inconsistencies by overriding default user-agent stylesheets, ensuring elements look consistent across different browsers.
